---
title: "NTFS Forensics with The Sleuth Kit"
subtitle: "CSCI 4623 — Digital Forensics"
format: html
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
number-sections: false
code-copy: true
---

## Overview

**Image:** `ntfs-lab.dd` (created by `setup_ntfs_lab.sh`)

**Tools:** TSK (`fsstat`, `fls`, `istat`, `icat`, `blkstat`, `blkcat`,
`ffind`, `ifind`, `tsk_recover`), `analyzeMFT`

**Assumed knowledge:** FAT and ext filesystem forensics, TSK command
basics (`fls`, `istat`, `icat`), partition analysis with `mmls`.

### Case background

Your team has received a forensic image of workstation WS-042, assigned
to contractor J. Smith (badge C-1847). Smith's access was revoked at
03:00 UTC on 2024-03-11 after network monitoring flagged repeated
connections to 192.168.1.88, a host that does not appear in the corporate
asset inventory and is not associated with any authorised service.
Preliminary evidence suggests that sensitive HR data — including a full
payroll export — was copied to this unregistered internal host in the
two hours preceding revocation.

Your task is to examine the NTFS volume for corroborating evidence:
confirm what files were present, how they were stored, whether any traces
were deliberately concealed, and what the filesystem's own journals
record about activity in that window.

### How NTFS differs from ext and FAT

| Concept | FAT | ext | NTFS |
|---|---|---|---|
| File metadata | Directory entry | Inode | MFT entry |
| Metadata location | In directory | Inode table | `$MFT` file |
| Small file data | Cluster | Block | Resident `$DATA` (in MFT) |
| Named data streams | No | No | Yes (`$DATA:streamname`) |
| Change journal | No | `$LogFile` (limited) | `$UsnJrnl` (full history) |
| Timestamps per file | 2 | 3 | 8 (4 in `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` + 4 in `$FILE_NAME`) |

Every object in NTFS — including the filesystem metadata itself — is a
file with a record in the `$MFT`. System files occupy MFT entries 0–11
and are named with a leading `$`.

---

## Task 0 · Setup and Orientation

Before examining individual files, establish the geometry of the volume:
sector size, cluster size, MFT location, and total addressable range.
These values anchor every subsequent address you will encounter — a
cluster number only means something once you know how large a cluster is
and where they start.

### 0.1 — Identify the image

```bash
file ntfs-lab.dd
```

```
ntfs-lab.dd: DOS/MBR boot sector, code offset 0x52+2,
  OEM-ID "NTFS    ", sectors/cluster 8, Media descriptor 0xf8,
  sectors/track 63, heads 255, hidden sectors 32,
  dos < 4.0 BootSector (0), FAT (1Y bit by descriptor); NTFS,
  sectors/track 63, physical drive 0x80, sectors 1009631,
  $MFT start cluster 42068, $MFTMirr start cluster 2,
  bytes/RecordSegment 2^(-1*246), clusters/index block 1,
  serial number 0e1890f81890e04f; contains bootstrap BOOTMGR
```

`file` reads the NTFS boot sector (`$Boot`, MFT entry 7) and surfaces
the key layout parameters. The `contains bootstrap BOOTMGR` annotation
indicates this is a genuine Windows-formatted NTFS volume — the boot
sector contains real Windows bootstrap code, not the stub written by
`mkntfs`. This is the common case when imaging a volume that was
formatted by Windows rather than by Linux NTFS tooling.

The image is a bare volume image: it begins at byte 0 of the NTFS
filesystem with no preceding partition table or MBR. There are no
partition offsets to account for when passing it to TSK tools.

Note the MFT location: start cluster 42068. Windows places the MFT zone
deep in the volume (here roughly one-third of the way in) and reserves
that region for MFT expansion, rather than placing the MFT at the
volume's beginning as `mkntfs` does. The `$MFTMirr` sits at cluster 2,
near the volume start, providing a redundant copy of the first few MFT
entries in a location unlikely to be damaged by the same event that
corrupts the primary MFT.

### 0.2 — Filesystem statistics

```bash
fsstat ntfs-lab.dd
```

```
FILE SYSTEM INFORMATION
--------------------------------------------
File System Type: NTFS
Volume Serial Number: 0E1890F81890E04F
OEM Name: NTFS
Volume Name: CSCI_4623
Version: Windows XP

METADATA INFORMATION
--------------------------------------------
First Cluster of MFT: 42068
First Cluster of MFT Mirror: 2
Size of MFT Entries: 1024 bytes
Size of Index Records: 4096 bytes
Range: 0 - 256
Root Directory: 5

CONTENT INFORMATION
--------------------------------------------
Sector Size: 512
Cluster Size: 4096
Total Cluster Range: 0 - 126202
Total Sector Range: 0 - 1009630
```

Work through the output in two groups. The metadata section tells you
where the MFT lives (cluster 42068) and how large each record is
(1024 bytes). `Range: 0 - 256` means 256 MFT entries have been
allocated on this volume (entries 0 through 255; entry 256 is the
virtual `$OrphanFiles` container TSK generates synthetically). The
content section tells you the addressable unit for data: a 4096-byte
cluster spanning 8 sectors. Every block address you see in run lists
and `blkstat` output is a cluster number; multiply by 4096 to get a
byte offset into the image.

The volume label `CSCI_4623` identifies the lab image. In ext, the
inode table location is a fixed block address recorded in the
superblock. In NTFS, there is no separate inode table — the MFT is
itself a file whose run list (stored in its own `$DATA` attribute)
tells TSK where MFT records are on disk.

---

## Task 1 · NTFS Metadata Files

In ext, `fsstat` points you to a fixed inode table. In NTFS, the
equivalent metadata is distributed across a set of special files, each
with a reserved MFT entry number. Before examining individual user files,
identify these system files and understand what each one contains — they
will surface repeatedly as you trace evidence.

### 1.1 — List the root directory

```bash
fls ntfs-lab.dd | sort -nk 2
```

```
r/r 0-128-6:    $MFT
r/r 1-128-1:    $MFTMirr
r/r 2-128-1:    $LogFile
r/r 3-128-3:    $Volume
r/r 4-128-1:    $AttrDef
r/r 6-128-4:    $Bitmap
r/r 7-128-1:    $Boot
r/r 8-128-1:    $BadClus:$Bad
r/r 8-128-2:    $BadClus
r/r 9-128-8:    $Secure:$SDS
r/r 9-144-11:   $Secure:$SDH
r/r 9-144-5:    $Secure:$SII
r/r 10-128-1:   $UpCase
r/r 10-128-4:   $UpCase:$Info
d/d 11-144-4:   $Extend
d/d 36-144-1:   System Volume Information
d/d 64-144-2:   Users
d/d 74-144-2:   System
V/V 256:        $OrphanFiles
```

The type prefix encodes two fields separated by `/`: the directory-entry
type and the inode type. `r/r` means the directory entry is a regular
file and the MFT entry is also typed as a regular file. `d/d` is a true
directory (entries with `$INDEX_ROOT`). `V/V` is a virtual TSK-internal
entry with no backing MFT record — `$OrphanFiles` is a synthetic
container TSK creates to hold deleted files whose parent directory can
no longer be determined. On this image `$OrphanFiles` has no children:
all deleted files retained resolvable parent pointer chains at
acquisition time.

Several system files carry multiple named streams that appear as separate
`fls` entries sharing an MFT number. `$BadClus` has a `:$Bad` stream
listing bad clusters and a default (empty) `$DATA` stream. `$Secure` has
three streams (`$SDS`, `$SDH`, `$SII`) implementing the security
descriptor database; `$SDH` and `$SII` are `$INDEX_ROOT` attributes
(type 144), which is why their attribute type differs. `$UpCase` carries
`:$Info` alongside the main uppercase table.

The attribute instance IDs (the third component of addresses like
`0-128-6` or `9-128-8`) differ from those on a `mkntfs`-created volume
because this volume was formatted by Windows, which allocates attribute
instances in its own sequence. The instance ID is an internal identifier
within the MFT entry; the TSK address you use for `icat` is read
directly from `fls` output rather than derived from first principles.

`System Volume Information` (entry 36) is a Windows-managed metadata
directory not present on `mkntfs`-formatted images. It stores Windows
Search index metadata, restore point data, and other OS bookkeeping.

MFT entry numbers for the system files (0–11) are fixed by the NTFS
specification. Entry numbers for user directories (`Users` at 64,
`System` at 74) are assigned at creation time and will vary between
images.

### 1.2 — Examine $MFT (entry 0)

```bash
istat ntfs-lab.dd 0
```

```
MFT Entry Header Values:
Entry: 0        Sequence: 1
$LogFile Sequence Number: 2102627
Allocated File
Links: 1

$STANDARD_INFORMATION Attribute Values:
Flags: Hidden, System
Owner ID: 0
Security ID: 256  ()
Created:        2026-03-11 11:31:50.226435500 (CDT)
File Modified:  2026-03-11 11:31:50.226435500 (CDT)
MFT Modified:   2026-03-11 11:31:50.226435500 (CDT)
Accessed:       2026-03-11 11:31:50.226435500 (CDT)

$FILE_NAME Attribute Values:
Flags: Hidden, System
Name: $MFT
Parent MFT Entry: 5   Sequence: 5
Allocated Size: 16384        Actual Size: 16384
Created:        2026-03-11 11:31:50.226435500 (CDT)
File Modified:  2026-03-11 11:31:50.226435500 (CDT)
MFT Modified:   2026-03-11 11:31:50.226435500 (CDT)
Accessed:       2026-03-11 11:31:50.226435500 (CDT)

Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 72
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-3)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 74
Type: $DATA (128-6)                   Name: N/A   Non-Resident  size: 262144  init_size: 262144
  42068 42069 42070 42071 42072 42073 42074 42075
  42076 42077 42078 42079 42080 42081 42082 42083
  42084 42085 42086 42087 42088 42089 42090 42091
  ...
  42124 42125 42126 42127 42128 42129 42130 42131
Type: $BITMAP (176-5)                 Name: N/A   Non-Resident  size: 4104  init_size: 4104
  42067 37
```

Two things in this output warrant attention before moving on.

First, the `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` and `$FILE_NAME` timestamps are
identical and both fall on 2026-03-11 at volume creation time. Security
ID is 256 (non-zero), indicating that `$MFT`'s security descriptor is
stored centrally in `$Secure` (entry 9) and referenced by ID — the
expected behavior on a genuine Windows-formatted NTFS volume.

Second, the `$DATA` attribute of `$MFT` *is* the MFT itself — 262,144
bytes = 256 MFT entries × 1024 bytes each, stored in 64 contiguous
clusters (42068–42131). The `init_size` matches `size`, indicating all
allocated clusters have been written. The `$BITMAP` attribute tracks
which of those 256 entries are in use; it is itself non-resident,
spanning two non-contiguous clusters (42067 and 37) — fragmentation
visible even at the metadata level. The structure that describes all
files on the volume is itself described as an entry within that same
structure.

### 1.3 — Pull the volume name from $Volume

```bash
istat ntfs-lab.dd 3
```

```
Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 72
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-1)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 80
Type: $VOLUME_NAME (96-4)             Name: N/A   Resident   size: 18
Type: $VOLUME_INFORMATION (112-5)     Name: N/A   Resident   size: 12
Type: $DATA (128-3)                   Name: N/A   Resident   size: 0
```

The `$VOLUME_NAME` attribute (type 96) holds the label as a UTF-16LE
string. Extract it with `icat ntfs-lab.dd 3-96-4 | strings -e l`
(`-e l` tells `strings` to scan for little-endian 16-bit characters;
the default ASCII mode will produce no output). The 18-byte attribute
encodes the 9-character label `CSCI_4623` (9 characters × 2 bytes each).
The `$VOLUME_INFORMATION` attribute records the NTFS version and a flags
field that can indicate a dirty (uncleanly unmounted) volume — relevant
because a dirty flag suggests the system crashed or was powered off
without a clean shutdown, which affects journal integrity.

### 1.4 — System file reference table

| TSK address | Name | Forensic relevance |
|---|---|---|
| `0-128-6` | `$MFT` | Master File Table — source of all metadata |
| `1-128-1` | `$MFTMirr` | First 4 MFT entries mirrored for recovery |
| `2-128-1` | `$LogFile` | Metadata redo/undo journal |
| `3-128-3` | `$Volume` | Volume name, NTFS version, dirty flag |
| `4-128-1` | `$AttrDef` | Attribute type definitions |
| `5-144-2` | `.` | Root directory (visible only with `fls -a`) |
| `6-128-4` | `$Bitmap` | One bit per cluster — allocated vs. free |
| `7-128-1` | `$Boot` | Boot sector (first 8 KB of volume) |
| `8-128-1` | `$BadClus:$Bad` | Named stream listing bad cluster runs |
| `8-128-2` | `$BadClus` | Default (empty) `$DATA` stream |
| `9-128-8` | `$Secure:$SDS` | Security Descriptor Stream — raw SD storage |
| `9-144-11` | `$Secure:$SDH` | Hash index over security descriptors |
| `9-144-5` | `$Secure:$SII` | ID index mapping security IDs to descriptors |
| `10-128-1` | `$UpCase` | Unicode uppercase table for case-insensitive comparison |
| `10-128-4` | `$UpCase:$Info` | Checksum and version metadata for the table |
| `11-144-4` | `$Extend` | Directory containing `$UsnJrnl`, `$Quota`, `$ObjId`, `$RmMetadata` |
| `36-144-1` | `System Volume Information` | Windows OS metadata (Search index, restore points) |
| `39` | `$UsnJrnl` | Update Sequence Number journal (`$J` stream at `39-128-3`) |
| `64-144-2` | `Users` | User profile directory tree |
| `74-144-2` | `System` | System log directory |
| `V/V 256` | `$OrphanFiles` | Virtual TSK container for parentless deleted files |

The three streams of `$Secure` together implement NTFS's centralised
security model. Prior to Windows 2000, each file stored its own security
descriptor inline in its MFT entry. `$Secure` consolidates all
descriptors in one place so that files sharing identical permissions
reference the same stored descriptor rather than duplicating it.

- **`$SDS` (Security Descriptor Stream):** The raw storage backing store.
  Every security descriptor on the volume is written sequentially into
  this stream as a self-describing record containing the header, owner
  SID, group SID, DACL (Discretionary Access Control List), and SACL
  (System ACL). Records are padded to 16-byte alignment and are never
  deleted — the stream only grows. Each file's MFT entry stores a
  Security ID (an integer) in its `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` attribute
  rather than the full descriptor; that ID is the key used to look up
  the actual descriptor in `$SDS`.

- **`$SDH` (Security Descriptor Hash index):** A B-tree index (attribute
  type 144, `$INDEX_ROOT`) keyed on a CRC32 hash of each security
  descriptor's content. Its purpose is deduplication: when a new
  descriptor is about to be written, Windows first hashes it, looks it
  up in `$SDH`, and — if the full descriptor matches an existing entry —
  reuses the existing Security ID rather than writing a duplicate. From a
  forensic perspective `$SDH` is rarely inspected directly, but its
  presence explains why the Security IDs you see in `istat` output are
  integers rather than ACLs.

- **`$SII` (Security ID Index):** A B-tree index keyed on Security ID
  (the integer stored in each file's `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`). Given a
  Security ID, `$SII` returns the byte offset of the corresponding record
  in `$SDS`, enabling fast lookup of a file's permissions at open time.
  Forensically, `$SII` is the starting point if you want to resolve a
  Security ID from `istat` output back to an actual ACL — iterate `$SII`
  to find the `$SDS` offset, then parse the descriptor at that offset.

---

## Task 2 · MFT Entry Anatomy

An MFT entry is NTFS's equivalent of an inode, but where an inode is a
fixed-length structure with named fields, an MFT entry is a variable-
length sequence of typed attributes. The attribute type code determines
what the attribute contains; the resident/non-resident flag determines
where the content lives. Learning to read `istat` output as an attribute
list — rather than as a flat set of fields — is the core skill for NTFS
forensics.

### 2.1 — Recursive listing

```bash
fls -r ntfs-lab.dd
```

```
d/d 64-144-2:   Users
+ d/d 65-144-2:     jsmith
++ d/d 68-144-2:        AppData
+++ d/d 69-144-2:           Roaming
++++ d/d 70-144-2:              Microsoft
+++++ d/d 71-144-2:                 Windows
++++++ d/d 72-144-2:                     Recent
++++ r/r 80-128-2:              sync_config.ini
++++ r/r 81-128-2:              sync_config.ini:credentials
++ d/d 67-144-2:        Desktop
+++ r/r 82-128-2:           project_notes.txt
+++ -/r * 96-128-2:         staging.txt
+++ -/r * 104-128-2:        draft_note.txt
++ d/d 66-144-2:        Documents
+++ r/r 83-128-2:           contracts_index.txt
+++ r/r 76-128-2:           incident_summary.txt
+++ r/r 77-128-2:           payroll_export.csv
+++ -/r * 88-128-2:         final_export.csv
+++ -/r * 112-128-2:        payroll_export_backup.csv
++ d/d 73-144-2:        Downloads
+++ r/r 78-128-2:           VPN_client_setup.exe
+++ r/r 79-128-2:           VPN_client_setup.exe:Zone.Identifier
r/r 4-128-1:    $AttrDef
r/r 8-128-2:    $BadClus
r/r 8-128-1:    $BadClus:$Bad
r/r 6-128-4:    $Bitmap
r/r 7-128-1:    $Boot
d/d 11-144-4:   $Extend
+ d/d 29-144-2:     $Deleted
+ r/r 25-144-2:     $ObjId:$O
+ r/r 24-144-3:     $Quota:$O
+ r/r 24-144-2:     $Quota:$Q
+ r/r 26-144-2:     $Reparse:$R
+ d/d 27-144-2:     $RmMetadata
++ r/r 28-128-4:        $Repair
++ r/r 28-128-2:        $Repair:$Config
++ d/d 31-144-2:        $Txf
++ d/d 30-144-2:        $TxfLog
+++ r/r 32-128-2:           $Tops
+++ r/r 32-128-4:           $Tops:$T
+++ r/r 33-128-1:           $TxfLog.blf
+++ r/r 34-128-1:           $TxfLogContainer00000000000000000001
+++ r/r 35-128-1:           $TxfLogContainer00000000000000000002
+ r/r 39-128-3:     $UsnJrnl:$J
+ r/r 39-128-4:     $UsnJrnl:$Max
r/r 2-128-1:    $LogFile
r/r 0-128-6:    $MFT
r/r 1-128-1:    $MFTMirr
r/r 9-128-8:    $Secure:$SDS
r/r 9-144-11:   $Secure:$SDH
r/r 9-144-5:    $Secure:$SII
r/r 10-128-1:   $UpCase
r/r 10-128-4:   $UpCase:$Info
r/r 3-128-3:    $Volume
d/d 74-144-2:   System
+ d/d 75-144-2:     Logs
++ -/r * 84-128-2:      access.log
d/d 36-144-1:   System Volume Information
+ r/r 38-128-1:     IndexerVolumeGuid
+ r/r 37-128-1:     WPSettings.dat
V/V 256:        $OrphanFiles
```

The inode address format `78-128-2` encodes three values: MFT entry 78,
attribute type 128 (`$DATA`), attribute instance ID 2. Named streams
(`Zone.Identifier`, `credentials`) appear as separate lines with
consecutive entry numbers rather than the same one — `79-128-2` for the
`Zone.Identifier` stream on the file whose default stream is at
`78-128-2`. This is the behavior of ntfs-3g when creating files with
named streams on Linux: it allocates a fresh MFT entry for each named
stream. Importantly, that separate entry is a fully independent MFT
record with its own `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`, `$FILE_NAME`,
`$SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR`, and a default (unnamed) `$DATA` attribute. The
stream name is embedded in the `$FILE_NAME` value using colon notation
(`sync_config.ini:credentials`). On a genuine Windows NTFS volume, all
named streams for a file always reside as multiple `$DATA` attributes
inside the same MFT entry. The forensic consequence is the same either
way: `icat` with the stream entry's TSK address recovers the hidden
content. You will examine both ADS entries in detail in Task 4.

Several details in the `$Extend` subtree are worth noting. `$UsnJrnl`
is visible here at entry 39 with two named streams: `$J` (change
records, address `39-128-3`) and `$Max` (journal configuration, address
`39-128-4`). You will examine the journal in Task 6. The `$RmMetadata`
directory and its `$TxfLog` subtree are Windows Transactional NTFS (TxF)
artifacts — they support the Windows kernel's transactional filesystem
API and are present on any Windows-formatted NTFS volume regardless of
whether applications use TxF explicitly.

Five deleted files appear in the listing under their original paths
(marked `-/r *`): `staging.txt` (96), `draft_note.txt` (104),
`final_export.csv` (88), `payroll_export_backup.csv` (112), and
`access.log` (84). All have resolvable parent directory chains, which is
why none appear under `$OrphanFiles`. The `-/r` prefix encodes the
deletion state: `-` means the parent directory index entry has been
removed, `r` means the MFT entry is still present and typed as a regular
file.

### 2.2 — Inspect incident_summary.txt

```bash
istat ntfs-lab.dd 76
```

```
MFT Entry Header Values:
Entry: 76        Sequence: 1
$LogFile Sequence Number: 0
Allocated File
Links: 1

$STANDARD_INFORMATION Attribute Values:
Flags: Archive
Owner ID: 0
Security ID: 0  ()
Created:        2026-03-11 12:55:31.553776600 (CDT)
File Modified:  2026-03-11 12:55:31.562745800 (CDT)
MFT Modified:   2026-03-11 12:55:31.562745800 (CDT)
Accessed:       2026-03-11 12:55:32.423947300 (CDT)

$FILE_NAME Attribute Values:
Flags: Archive
Name: incident_summary.txt
Parent MFT Entry: 66   Sequence: 1
Allocated Size: 336        Actual Size: 0

Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 48
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-3)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 106
Type: $SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR (80-1)     Name: N/A   Resident   size: 80
Type: $DATA (128-2)                   Name: N/A   Resident   size: 335
```

Walk through the attribute list. Four attributes are present; two are
worth pausing on.

`$SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR` (type 80) appears here as a resident inline
attribute, and the Security ID in `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` is `0`. This
is a Linux ntfs-3g artifact: when files are created through the ntfs-3g
FUSE driver, the driver writes security descriptors inline into the MFT
entry rather than registering them in `$Secure`. The system files (entry
0, entry 3) show non-zero Security IDs and no inline `$SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR`
because those were initialised by Windows at format time. On a real case
image where all writes were performed by Windows, all files would show
non-zero Security IDs and no inline `$SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR` attribute —
its presence here is a reliable indicator that this data was written via
Linux NTFS tooling.

`$FILE_NAME` (type 48, instance 3) records a `Parent MFT Entry: 66`,
which is the `Documents` directory — this is how NTFS reconstructs
paths even for deleted files whose directory entry has been cleared.
Note that the FN `Actual Size` field shows `0` while the `$DATA`
attribute records 335 bytes. The FN size field is populated by the
NTFS kernel driver during directory index updates; ntfs-3g does not
always synchronise it at write time. The authoritative file size is
always the `$DATA` attribute's recorded size.

### 2.3 — Attribute type reference

| Type code | Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 16 (0x10) | `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` | Timestamps, flags, owner/security IDs |
| 48 (0x30) | `$FILE_NAME` | Name, parent entry, second timestamp set |
| 80 (0x50) | `$SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR` | Inline security descriptor (ntfs-3g artifact) |
| 96 (0x60) | `$VOLUME_NAME` | Volume label string |
| 112 (0x70) | `$VOLUME_INFORMATION` | NTFS version, dirty flag |
| 128 (0x80) | `$DATA` | File content (resident or non-resident) |
| 144 (0x90) | `$INDEX_ROOT` | Directory B-tree root node |
| 160 (0xA0) | `$INDEX_ALLOCATION` | Directory B-tree non-resident overflow |
| 176 (0xB0) | `$BITMAP` | Allocation tracking for directories and `$MFT` |

---

## Task 3 · Resident vs. Non-Resident Attributes

When a file's data fits within the available space in its 1024-byte MFT
record, NTFS stores it inline as a *resident* attribute — no cluster
allocation occurs at all. The content lives entirely inside the MFT.
When data is too large, NTFS marks the `$DATA` attribute *non-resident*
and stores a *run list* (an extent map) in its place: a compact encoding
of which clusters hold the content, and in what order.

This distinction matters forensically for two reasons. Resident data
survives as long as the MFT entry survives, even if the surrounding
clusters have been reallocated. Non-resident data can be partially
overwritten cluster by cluster, creating recovery scenarios where some
extents are intact and others are not.

Smith's manager has claimed the payroll export was too large to have been
typed or pasted manually. Confirming its non-resident status — and
locating its cluster run — is the first step in verifying that claim and
establishing that the data was physically present on this volume.

### 3.1 — Resident: incident_summary.txt

From Task 2, `$DATA` is resident at 335 bytes. Confirm the content is
returned directly from the MFT with no cluster lookup:

```bash
icat ntfs-lab.dd 76
```

```
INTERNAL — RESTRICTED
Case: 2024-0311
Subject: J. Smith, contractor, badge C-1847
Access revoked: 2024-03-11 03:00 UTC
Summary: Three files confirmed copied to unregistered host 192.168.1.88
between 02:14 and 02:31 UTC on 2024-03-11. No malware identified.
Assigned analyst: review this workstation image for corroborating evidence.
```

The data was returned directly from the MFT record. No cluster lookup
was required. If the surrounding clusters had been reallocated — or if
the file had never been written to clusters at all — `icat` would still
return this content as long as the MFT entry itself was intact.

### 3.2 — Non-resident: payroll_export.csv

```bash
istat ntfs-lab.dd 77
```

```
MFT Entry Header Values:
Entry: 77        Sequence: 1
$LogFile Sequence Number: 0
Allocated File
Links: 1

$STANDARD_INFORMATION Attribute Values:
Flags: Archive
Owner ID: 0
Security ID: 0  ()
Created:        2026-03-11 12:55:32.005800300 (CDT)
File Modified:  2026-03-11 12:55:32.377868400 (CDT)
MFT Modified:   2026-03-11 12:55:32.377868400 (CDT)
Accessed:       2026-03-11 12:55:35.011026900 (CDT)

$FILE_NAME Attribute Values:
Flags: Archive
Name: payroll_export.csv
Parent MFT Entry: 66   Sequence: 1
Allocated Size: 856064        Actual Size: 0

Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 48
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-3)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 102
Type: $SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR (80-1)     Name: N/A   Resident   size: 80
Type: $DATA (128-2)                   Name: N/A   Non-Resident  size: 854222  init_size: 854222
  57840 57841 57842 57843 57844 57845 57846 57847
  57848 57849 57850 57851 57852 57853 57854 57855
  57856 57857 57858 57859 57860 57861 57862 57863
  ...
  58040 58041 58042 58043 58044 58045 58046 58047
  58048
```

The `$DATA` attribute is non-resident — 854,222 bytes is too large for
the 1,024-byte MFT record. Its content field is a run list: a compact
encoding of which clusters hold the data. The run is contiguous from
cluster 57840 through 58048, a span of 209 clusters. The `init_size`
field matches `size`, indicating all allocated clusters have been
written (no sparse holes).

As with entry 76, `$FILE_NAME` shows `Actual Size: 0` — a ntfs-3g
artifact; the authoritative size is always the `$DATA` attribute's
recorded value of 854,222 bytes.

### 3.3 — Verify cluster geometry

Before reading from the cluster run, calculate the expected cluster count
independently to confirm the run list is consistent with the recorded
file size:

```bash
python3 -c "import math; print(math.ceil(854222 / 4096))"
```

```
209
```

The run list reports 209 contiguous clusters (57840–58048), matching the
ceiling exactly. The last cluster holds 854222 − (208 × 4096) = 2574
bytes of file data; the remaining 4096 − 2574 = 1522 bytes are cluster
slack. Now confirm the first cluster of the run holds what we expect:

```bash
blkcat ntfs-lab.dd 57840 | head -c 80 | xxd
```

```
00000000: 456d 706c 6f79 6565 4944 2c46 6972 7374  EmployeeID,First
00000010: 4e61 6d65 2c4c 6173 744e 616d 652c 4465  Name,LastName,De
00000020: 7061 7274 6d65 6e74 2c53 616c 6172 792c  partment,Salary,
00000030: 5353 4e5f 4c61 7374 342c 4261 6e6b 526f  SSN_Last4,BankRo
00000040: 7574 696e 674c 6173 7434 0d0a 4530 3030  utingLast4..E000
```

The cluster begins with the CSV header row, confirming this is the start
of `payroll_export.csv`. The HR data was physically present on this
volume and is directly recoverable from the cluster run.

```bash
blkstat ntfs-lab.dd 57840
```

```
Cluster: 57840
Allocated
```

`blkstat` correctly reports `Allocated` for this cluster — it is in use
by `payroll_export.csv` and is not available for new file allocation.

---

## Task 4 · Alternate Data Streams

A named `$DATA` attribute — called an Alternate Data Stream (ADS) —
allows a single MFT entry to carry multiple independent data payloads.
The filename visible to users and most tools refers only to the default
(unnamed) `$DATA` attribute. Named streams are invisible to `ls`, `cat`,
`wc`, and forensic keyword search passes that iterate over default streams
only. The file size reported by the directory index reflects only the
default stream.

On Windows, the NTFS driver uses a stream named `Zone.Identifier` on
files downloaded from the Internet to record provenance: the download URL,
referring page, and security zone. This is legitimate and forensically
valuable. Malicious use of ADS follows the same mechanism: store a payload
in a named stream on an innocuous-looking file, then access it with a
stream-qualified path.

The image contains two ADS artifacts. One records where `VPN_client_setup.exe`
was downloaded from. The other, on `sync_config.ini`, may explain the
exfiltration mechanism.

### 4.1 — Identify ADS in fls output

```bash
fls -r ntfs-lab.dd | grep ':.*:'
```

```
r/r 79-128-2:   Users/jsmith/Downloads/VPN_client_setup.exe:Zone.Identifier
r/r 81-128-2:   Users/jsmith/AppData/Roaming/sync_config.ini:credentials
```

Both stream entries have entry numbers adjacent to their parent file
(`79` follows `78`; `81` follows `80`), confirming the ntfs-3g behavior
noted in Task 2: named streams are allocated to consecutive MFT entries.
The `icat` address uses the stream entry's own number directly.

### 4.2 — Examine attribute layout with istat

```bash
istat ntfs-lab.dd 80
istat ntfs-lab.dd 81
```

```
Entry 80 — sync_config.ini (default stream):
Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 48
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-3)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 96
Type: $SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR (80-1)     Name: N/A   Resident   size: 80
Type: $DATA (128-2)                   Name: N/A   Resident   size: 75

Entry 81 — sync_config.ini:credentials (named stream):
Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 48
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-3)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 120
Type: $SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR (80-1)     Name: N/A   Resident   size: 80
Type: $DATA (128-2)                   Name: N/A   Resident   size: 67
```

Because ntfs-3g allocates named streams to separate MFT entries, the
`credentials` stream occupies its own fully independent entry (81) with
a complete set of attributes. The `$FILE_NAME` for entry 81 records the
name as `sync_config.ini:credentials` (120 bytes = 30 UTF-16LE
characters + 60-byte base) — the stream name is embedded directly in
the filename field using colon notation. The `$DATA` in entry 81 is that
entry's default (unnamed) stream; it contains the credentials content.
The TSK address for extraction is therefore `81-128-2`.

On a genuine Windows NTFS volume, this structure would look entirely
different: both `$DATA` attributes — the default stream and the named
`:credentials` stream — would reside inside a single MFT entry (entry
80), each with its own attribute instance ID. The stream-qualified address
would encode the named `$DATA` attribute's instance ID directly within
entry 80. The presence of a fully populated separate MFT entry for the
stream, with `$FILE_NAME` carrying the colon-encoded stream name, is a
reliable indicator that this ADS was written via the ntfs-3g driver on
Linux.

### 4.3 — Extract the default stream

```bash
icat ntfs-lab.dd 80
```

```
[SyncTool v2.1]
server=backup.internal.corp
interval=3600
compression=true
```

Benign-looking configuration. No indication of exfiltration tooling from
the default stream alone.

### 4.4 — Extract the credentials stream

> **↳ Your task:** Extract the `credentials` named stream from
> `sync_config.ini`. What does the `remote` field contain, and how does
> it connect to the network alert?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### Answer

The stream's content lives in the default `$DATA` of MFT entry 81.
Read it directly using the entry's TSK address:

```bash
icat ntfs-lab.dd 81-128-2
```

```
api_key=sk-4623-EXFIL-a7f3c9d1e2b8
remote=sftp://192.168.1.88/drop
```

The remote SFTP destination matches the IP address in the network
monitoring alert. The API key provides authentication. Together they
describe a configured exfiltration channel: a sync tool pointed at an
unregistered internal host, with credentials stored in a stream
invisible to standard directory listings.

On a real case image from a Windows system, the named stream would
reside inside the file's own MFT entry alongside the default `$DATA`.
In both cases, the correct extraction address comes from reading the
`fls -r` output directly — the listing shows you the exact TSK address
to pass to `icat`.
:::

### 4.5 — Examine the Zone.Identifier stream

> **↳ Your task:** Extract the `Zone.Identifier` stream from
> `VPN_client_setup.exe` (TSK address `79-128-2`). What do the `ZoneId`,
> `ReferrerUrl`, and `HostUrl` fields tell you about where this file
> came from? Why is this stream a more reliable provenance indicator
> than the file's SI timestamps?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### Answer

```bash
icat ntfs-lab.dd 79-128-2
```

```
[ZoneTransfer]
ZoneId=3
ReferrerUrl=http://transfer.sh/upload
HostUrl=http://transfer.sh/VPN_client_setup.exe
```

`ZoneId=3` is the Windows Internet zone — the file was downloaded from
the Internet rather than a local or intranet source. The `HostUrl`
identifies a public file-transfer service, not an internal software
distribution point. The Windows shell writes this stream automatically
at download time through the `IAttachmentExecute` interface; it cannot
be set retroactively through normal file operations. This makes it
substantially harder to fabricate after the fact than SI timestamps,
which are writable by any process.
:::

---

## Task 5 · Deletion and Recovery

When a file is deleted in NTFS, the MFT entry is marked unallocated and
the clusters belonging to its `$DATA` attribute are cleared in `$Bitmap`.
The MFT record itself is not zeroed — the `$FILE_NAME`, `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`,
and (for resident files) `$DATA` attributes remain physically in place
until the entry is reused by a new file. For non-resident files, the
data clusters are preserved until reallocated.

TSK exploits this by scanning the MFT for entries where the allocated
flag is cleared, exposing deleted files that the filesystem no longer
acknowledges. The recovery window closes as new file activity reuses MFT
entries and clusters. On a live system under active use this window can
close within minutes; on a forensic image taken shortly after the incident,
recently deleted files are typically still recoverable.

### 5.1 — List unallocated entries

```bash
fls -r -d ntfs-lab.dd
```

```
-/r * 96-128-2:  Users/jsmith/Desktop/staging.txt
-/r * 104-128-2: Users/jsmith/Desktop/draft_note.txt
-/r * 88-128-2:  Users/jsmith/Documents/final_export.csv
-/r * 112-128-2: Users/jsmith/Documents/payroll_export_backup.csv
-/r * 84-128-2:  System/Logs/access.log
```

All five deleted files retain intact MFT entries with fully resolvable
paths. This is the best-case recovery scenario: the image was acquired
before any subsequent file creation could reuse these MFT entries. The
`$OrphanFiles` container has no children on this image — every deleted
entry's `$FILE_NAME` parent pointer chain leads back to an allocated
directory.

The type prefix `-/r` encodes two fields: `-` means no directory entry
exists for this file (the parent directory index entry was removed at
deletion time), `r` means the MFT entry is typed as a regular file.
Compare this with `r/r *`, which would mean a directory entry still
exists pointing to an unallocated inode — that indicates a
partially-cleaned deletion. The `-/r` form is the normal post-deletion
state in NTFS.

### 5.2 — Recover with tsk_recover

`tsk_recover` has two modes. Without flags it recovers only unallocated
files — deleted files whose MFT entries are still intact. With `-a` it
recovers all allocated files.

```bash
mkdir -p recovered
tsk_recover ntfs-lab.dd recovered/
tree recovered/
```

```
Files Recovered: 5
recovered/
├── System
│   └── Logs
│       └── access.log
└── Users
    └── jsmith
        ├── Desktop
        │   ├── draft_note.txt
        │   └── staging.txt
        └── Documents
            ├── final_export.csv
            └── payroll_export_backup.csv
```

All five deleted files are recovered under their original paths — the
intact MFT entries give TSK everything needed to reconstruct filenames,
parent directories, and content. `access.log` recovers fully even though
it was a non-resident file, because its data clusters had not been
reallocated before imaging.

Now run with `-a` to recover all allocated files:

```bash
mkdir -p recovered-a
tsk_recover -a ntfs-lab.dd recovered-a/
tree recovered-a/
```

```
Files Recovered: 14
recovered-a/
├── $Extend
│   └── $RmMetadata
│       └── $TxfLog
│           ├── $Tops
│           ├── $TxfLog.blf
│           ├── $TxfLogContainer00000000000000000001
│           └── $TxfLogContainer00000000000000000002
├── System Volume Information
│   ├── IndexerVolumeGuid
│   └── WPSettings.dat
└── Users
    └── jsmith
        ├── AppData
        │   └── Roaming
        │       ├── sync_config.ini
        │       └── sync_config.ini:credentials
        ├── Desktop
        │   └── project_notes.txt
        ├── Documents
        │   ├── contracts_index.txt
        │   ├── incident_summary.txt
        │   └── payroll_export.csv
        └── Downloads
            ├── VPN_client_setup.exe
            └── VPN_client_setup.exe:Zone.Identifier
```

Two things stand out in the `-a` output. First, the ADS streams
(`sync_config.ini:credentials` and `VPN_client_setup.exe:Zone.Identifier`)
appear alongside their parent files — `tsk_recover` writes each named
stream entry as an individual file, making hidden stream content
available without requiring a stream-qualified `icat` address. Second,
the `$Extend/$RmMetadata/$TxfLog` subtree and `System Volume Information`
appear — these are Windows metadata artifacts that were allocated and
are part of the live filesystem. The `-a` flag recovers all allocated
files including these system artifacts. Note that `-a` does not include
the deleted files — those require a separate invocation or the `-e` flag.

To recover everything in one pass:

```bash
mkdir -p recovered-e
tsk_recover -e ntfs-lab.dd recovered-e/
tree recovered-e/
```

```
Files Recovered: 19
recovered-e/
├── $Extend
│   └── $RmMetadata
│       └── $TxfLog
│           ├── $Tops
│           ├── $TxfLog.blf
│           ├── $TxfLogContainer00000000000000000001
│           └── $TxfLogContainer00000000000000000002
├── System
│   └── Logs
│       └── access.log
├── System Volume Information
│   ├── IndexerVolumeGuid
│   └── WPSettings.dat
└── Users
    └── jsmith
        ├── AppData
        │   └── Roaming
        │       ├── sync_config.ini
        │       └── sync_config.ini:credentials
        ├── Desktop
        │   ├── draft_note.txt
        │   ├── project_notes.txt
        │   └── staging.txt
        ├── Documents
        │   ├── contracts_index.txt
        │   ├── final_export.csv
        │   ├── incident_summary.txt
        │   ├── payroll_export_backup.csv
        │   └── payroll_export.csv
        └── Downloads
            ├── VPN_client_setup.exe
            └── VPN_client_setup.exe:Zone.Identifier
```

`-e` (everything) combines allocated and unallocated recovery in a
single pass — 19 files = 14 allocated + 5 deleted. This is the flag to
use in casework when you want a complete recovery image without running
separate invocations.

### 5.3 — Check recovery status for a resident deleted file

`draft_note.txt` was recovered above, but understanding *why* it was
recoverable is the point. It is small enough to have been stored
resident — meaning its content lived entirely inside the MFT record,
never in a data cluster.

> **↳ Your task:** Run `istat ntfs-lab.dd 104`. Does `$DATA` have a
> cluster run? What would have happened to this file's content if entry
> 104 had been reused before the image was acquired? How does resident
> storage differ from `access.log` in terms of recovery risk?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### Answer

```bash
istat ntfs-lab.dd 104
```

```
MFT Entry Header Values:
Entry: 104       Sequence: 2
Not Allocated File
Links: 0

Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 48
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-3)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 94
Type: $SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR (80-1)     Name: N/A   Resident   size: 80
Type: $DATA (128-2)                   Name: N/A   Resident   size: 6
```

`$DATA` is resident — no cluster run. The content ("draft\n") lives
inside the MFT record itself. A carving tool scanning unallocated
clusters would never find this file because it was never written to a
cluster. Recovery depends entirely on the MFT entry surviving intact.
Note `Sequence: 2` — this entry has been allocated and freed once; the
sequence counter increments at each reuse, allowing stale `$FILE_NAME`
parent references to be detected.

`access.log` was non-resident (its size exceeded the MFT record
capacity) and its content lived in data clusters. Both files survived
here because their MFT entries were not reused before imaging. The
difference is what fails first: for `draft_note.txt`, the only
recovery path is the MFT entry — if entry 104 had been reused, the
content would be gone entirely with no cluster-level backup. For
`access.log`, even if the MFT entry had been reused, cluster-level
carving might still recover the log content as long as those specific
clusters had not been overwritten.
:::

### 5.4 — The limits of MFT-based recovery

All five deleted files were recoverable on this image because it was
acquired promptly after the incident, before any new file creation could
reuse those MFT entries. This is not the typical situation in production
casework.

> **↳ Your task:** Suppose entries 84, 88, 96, 104, and 112 had all
> been reallocated to new files before imaging. What would `fls -r -d`
> show? What filesystem structure would still carry evidence of the
> rename from `temp_export.csv` to `final_export.csv`, and where in
> this tutorial will you find that structure?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### Answer

`fls -r -d` would return empty output — once an MFT entry is
reallocated, TSK's directory walker sees only the new file occupying
that entry and has no way to recover the prior occupant's metadata.
Deleted files with reused entries disappear from MFT-based listing
entirely.

The `$UsnJrnl` (Task 6) records every filesystem change event as a
sequential log, independent of whether MFT entries are still allocated.
A rename appears as a `RENAME_OLD_NAME` / `RENAME_NEW_NAME` pair tagged
with the file's MFT entry number and both the old and new filenames —
even after that MFT entry has been reused. The journal persists as long
as its own clusters have not been overwritten, making it the primary
evidence source for file activity that has been purged from the MFT.
:::

---

## Task 6 · Journaling and MFT Dump

NTFS maintains two journals. `$LogFile` (entry 2) is a redo/undo log for
metadata operations — its purpose is crash consistency, not investigative
history. It wraps quickly and its binary format is not designed for
forensic consumption.

`$UsnJrnl` (Update Sequence Number journal) is different. It records
every filesystem change — creation, deletion, rename, attribute modification,
data write — as a sequential stream of fixed-size records. It does not
store file content, only change events tagged with the filename and MFT
entry number of the affected file. On a production system it wraps after
a configurable size (default 32 MB on modern Windows), but the history
it preserves can span days or weeks.

### 6.1 — Locate $UsnJrnl under $Extend

```bash
fls ntfs-lab.dd 11
```

```
r/r 25-144-2:   $ObjId:$O
r/r 24-144-3:   $Quota:$O
r/r 24-144-2:   $Quota:$Q
r/r 26-144-2:   $Reparse:$R
d/d 29-144-2:   $Deleted
d/d 27-144-2:   $RmMetadata
r/r 39-128-3:   $UsnJrnl:$J
r/r 39-128-4:   $UsnJrnl:$Max
```

`$UsnJrnl` is directly visible in the `$Extend` directory listing at
entry 39 with two named streams: `$J` (the change records, address
`39-128-3`) and `$Max` (journal configuration, address `39-128-4`).
Both streams share the same MFT entry — unlike the ntfs-3g ADS
behavior seen in Task 4, `$UsnJrnl` was created by the Windows NTFS
driver and stores both named `$DATA` attributes within a single entry.

```bash
istat ntfs-lab.dd 39
```

```
$STANDARD_INFORMATION Attribute Values:
Flags: Hidden, System, Sparse

$FILE_NAME Attribute Values:
Name: $UsnJrnl
Parent MFT Entry: 11   Sequence: 11
Allocated Size: 0   Actual Size: 0

Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 72
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-1)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 82
Type: $DATA (128-3)    Name: $J   Non-Resident, Sparse   size: 176  init_size: 176
  1416 0 0 0 0 ...
Type: $DATA (128-4)    Name: $Max   Resident   size: 32
```

The `Sparse` flag on `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` and the `Non-Resident, Sparse`
annotation on `$J` indicate that the journal uses sparse allocation — the
logical file size is recorded as 0 in the `$FILE_NAME` even though data
clusters exist. This is the standard NTFS journal representation: the
sparse region at the beginning of `$J` represents the virtual address
space before the first written record. The actual change records begin at
cluster 1416.

### 6.2 — Extract and parse the $J stream

```bash
icat ntfs-lab.dd 39-128-3 > usn.bin
xxd usn.bin | head -20
```

```
00000000: 5800 0000 0200 0000 2100 0000 0000 0100  X.......!.......
00000010: 1e00 0000 0000 0100 0000 0000 0000 0000  ................
00000020: 0f67 0012 7fb1 dc01 0100 0000 0000 0000  .g..............
00000030: 0000 0000 2000 0000 1600 3c00 2400 5400  .... .....<.$.T.
00000040: 7800 6600 4c00 6f00 6700 2e00 6200 6c00  x.f.L.o.g...b.l.
00000050: 6600 0000 0000 0000                      f.......
```

The first USN record begins immediately at offset 0 — there is no
leading sparse region in the extracted bytes. The 4-byte record length
at offset 0 is `0x58` = 88 bytes. Parsing the record structure: major
version (offset 4) is 2, indicating USN v2 records. The filename length
field (offset 56) is `0x16` = 22 bytes (11 UTF-16LE characters), and
the bytes from offset 60 onward decode as `$TxfLog.blf` — the Windows
Transactional NTFS log file at MFT entry 33.

Parse all records to extract the event sequence:

```bash
python3 - << 'EOF'
import struct

REASONS = {
    0x00000001: "DATA_OVERWRITE",  0x00000002: "DATA_EXTEND",
    0x00000100: "FILE_CREATE",     0x00000200: "FILE_DELETE",
    0x00001000: "RENAME_OLD_NAME", 0x00002000: "RENAME_NEW_NAME",
    0x00004000: "INDEXABLE_CHANGE",0x80000000: "CLOSE",
}

data = open("usn.bin", "rb").read()
i = 0
while i < len(data) - 60:
    rec_len = struct.unpack_from("<I", data, i)[0]
    if rec_len < 60 or rec_len > 65536:
        i += 8
        continue
    reason  = struct.unpack_from("<I", data, i + 40)[0]
    fn_len  = struct.unpack_from("<H", data, i + 56)[0]
    fn_off  = struct.unpack_from("<H", data, i + 58)[0]
    name    = data[i+fn_off : i+fn_off+fn_len].decode("utf-16-le", errors="replace")
    reasons = "|".join(v for k,v in REASONS.items() if reason & k)
    print(f"  {reasons:<30} {name}")
    i += rec_len
EOF
```

```
  DATA_OVERWRITE                 $TxfLog.blf
  DATA_OVERWRITE|CLOSE           $TxfLog.blf
```

The journal contains only two records, both concerning `$TxfLog.blf` —
the Windows TxF internal log file that was updated as part of normal
volume housekeeping when the image was mounted.

None of the incident-related operations appear in the journal. A fully
populated journal would show records for every operation in the
exfiltration window: the creation and write of `temp_export.csv`, the
`RENAME_OLD_NAME` / `RENAME_NEW_NAME` pair when it was renamed to
`final_export.csv`, the deletion events for `access.log` and
`staging.txt`, and the copy and deletion of `payroll_export_backup.csv`.

The absence of these records is itself forensically significant. A
near-empty journal on a volume with visible file activity indicates one
of two things: the journal was explicitly cleared as an anti-forensic
measure, or — more commonly in lab and newly-provisioned environments —
the journal was initialised (via `fsutil usn createjournal`) after the
filesystem operations had already been performed, so those operations
were never captured. In a real case, a journal gap covering the precise
window of suspected activity would warrant noting in the case report and
may itself be evidence of deliberate interference.

The parsing code above is reusable regardless of journal size. When
examining a production image with a populated journal, the same script
will decode the full event sequence — the USN v2 record structure is
identical whether the journal holds two records or two million.

### 6.3 — Parse $MFT with analyzeMFT

`analyzeMFT` parses the raw `$MFT` bytes into a CSV with one row per
entry, including deleted and unallocated entries, with all attribute
fields decoded into readable columns.

> **↳ Your task:** Extract `$MFT`, run `analyzeMFT`, and check the
> output for the five deleted entries. What does the `In Use` column
> show, and how do the timestamps compare to what `istat` reported?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### Answer

```bash
icat ntfs-lab.dd 0 > mft.raw
analyzeMFT -f mft.raw -o mft.csv
grep -i "draft_note\|payroll_export_backup\|staging\|final_export\|access.log" mft.csv
```

The `In Use` column reads `False` for all five deleted entries —
confirming they are flagged as unallocated in the MFT bitmap despite
their records being physically present. All five files appear because
none of their MFT entries (84, 88, 96, 104, 112) had been reused
before acquisition. This is the contrast with the journal: the MFT
still holds the metadata for these files, but the journal does not hold
the operational history that created and then deleted them.

If `analyzeMFT` is not installed: `pip install analyzeMFT --break-system-packages`
:::

---

## Task 7 · Mini-Challenge — Timestomping

Anti-forensic timestomping exploits the fact that `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`
timestamps are writable by any process through normal Windows API calls —
a single call to `SetFileTime` can set them to any arbitrary value. The
`$FILE_NAME` timestamps, by contrast, are written by the NTFS kernel
driver when a directory index entry is created or updated. They do not
respond to user-space timestamp modification APIs.

When SI and FN timestamps diverge — especially when SI timestamps are
implausibly old while FN timestamps are consistent with surrounding files
— the divergence is a strong indicator of deliberate manipulation. This
is detectable with `istat` alone: it reports both sets in every MFT entry.

There are two timestomped files in the image. They are not equally
obvious — the second requires comparing individual timestamp fields
rather than spotting an obviously wrong year. Find both, and for each
document: the filename, the MFT entry number, which specific timestamps
diverge, the SI value, the FN value, and what forensic conclusion you
would draw about what the attacker was trying to accomplish.

::: {.callout-tip}
Run `istat` on each regular user file and compare the
`$STANDARD_INFORMATION` and `$FILE_NAME` blocks side by side. The two
anomalies have different signatures — one stomps SI Modified and
Accessed to 2001 while leaving SI Created intact, the other changes
only SI Modified to a different year. In both cases the FN timestamps
are the ground truth.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### Answer

**File 1: project_notes.txt (MFT entry 82)**

```bash
istat ntfs-lab.dd 82
```

```
$STANDARD_INFORMATION Attribute Values:
Created:        2026-03-11 12:55:32.423629400 (CDT)
File Modified:  2001-01-01 00:00:00.000000000 (CST)
MFT Modified:   2026-03-11 12:55:33.465006600 (CDT)
Accessed:       2001-01-01 00:00:00.000000000 (CST)

$FILE_NAME Attribute Values:
Created:        2026-03-11 12:55:32.423629400 (CDT)
File Modified:  2026-03-11 12:55:32.423629400 (CDT)
MFT Modified:   2026-03-11 12:55:32.423629400 (CDT)
Accessed:       2026-03-11 12:55:32.423629400 (CDT)
```

`touch -t` on Linux sets mtime and atime. Via ntfs-3g these map to SI
File Modified and SI Accessed — not SI Created or SI MFT Modified. SI
Created is unchanged from the actual file creation time; SI MFT Modified
is updated to the wall-clock time of the `touch` call itself. The
stomp is detectable on two dimensions: the 2001 values for Modified and
Accessed diverge from the FN timestamps, and the SI Created / MFT
Modified timestamps are internally inconsistent (both in 2026 while
Modified is in 2001). A tool that stomps SI timestamps on a real Windows
system would typically call `SetFileTime` which allows all four fields to
be set simultaneously — the Linux `touch` limitation means the lab stomp
is cruder than a real attacker's, but the FN divergence signal is
identical.

**File 2: contracts_index.txt (MFT entry 83)**

```bash
istat ntfs-lab.dd 83
```

```
$STANDARD_INFORMATION Attribute Values:
Created:        2026-03-11 12:55:33.473193500 (CDT)
File Modified:  2015-06-15 09:00:00.000000000 (CDT)
MFT Modified:   2026-03-11 12:55:34.508327300 (CDT)
Accessed:       2026-03-11 12:55:33.473193500 (CDT)

$FILE_NAME Attribute Values:
Created:        2026-03-11 12:55:33.473193500 (CDT)
File Modified:  2026-03-11 12:55:33.473193500 (CDT)
MFT Modified:   2026-03-11 12:55:33.473193500 (CDT)
Accessed:       2026-03-11 12:55:33.473193500 (CDT)
```

`touch -m` sets only mtime, mapping via ntfs-3g to SI File Modified
only. SI Created, Accessed, and MFT Modified are all untouched. The
divergence here is a single field: SI File Modified is 2015-06-15 while
the FN File Modified is 2026-03-11, and all other SI timestamps are
internally consistent with 2026. This is the subtler of the two stomps
— it requires checking each timestamp field individually rather than
spotting an obviously anomalous year across the whole block. The
forensic conclusion is the same: SI File Modified was deliberately set
to move this file's last-modified marker eleven years into the past,
potentially placing it outside the investigation window. The FN value
directly contradicts it.
:::

---

## Summary

| Task | Concept | Key commands |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | NTFS geometry, Windows-formatted volume | `fsstat`, `file` |
| 1 | System metadata files, `$Volume` attributes | `fls`, `istat 0`, `istat 3` |
| 2 | MFT entry anatomy, attribute types, ntfs-3g ADS layout | `istat`, `fls -r` |
| 3 | Resident vs. non-resident, run list geometry | `istat`, `blkstat`, `blkcat` |
| 4 | Alternate Data Streams, `Zone.Identifier` | `fls`, `icat <entry>-128-<id>` |
| 5 | Deletion, recovery states, tsk_recover modes | `fls -r -d`, `tsk_recover [-a] [-e]` |
| 6 | `$UsnJrnl` structure and parsing, journal gaps | `icat 39-128-3`, `analyzeMFT` |
| 7 | SI vs. FN timestamp divergence, timestomping | `istat` (compare both blocks) |

### Reference: NTFS system file MFT entries

| TSK address | Name | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `0-128-6` | `$MFT` | Master File Table |
| `1-128-1` | `$MFTMirr` | MFT mirror (first 4 entries) |
| `2-128-1` | `$LogFile` | Metadata redo/undo journal |
| `3-128-3` | `$Volume` | Volume name, version, dirty flag |
| `4-128-1` | `$AttrDef` | Attribute type definitions |
| `5-144-2` | `.` | Root directory |
| `6-128-4` | `$Bitmap` | Cluster allocation map |
| `7-128-1` | `$Boot` | Boot sector |
| `8-128-1` | `$BadClus:$Bad` | Bad cluster run list |
| `8-128-2` | `$BadClus` | Default empty stream |
| `9-128-8` | `$Secure:$SDS` | Security descriptor storage |
| `9-144-11` | `$Secure:$SDH` | Security descriptor hash index |
| `9-144-5` | `$Secure:$SII` | Security ID index |
| `10-128-1` | `$UpCase` | Unicode uppercase table |
| `10-128-4` | `$UpCase:$Info` | Uppercase table metadata |
| `11-144-4` | `$Extend` | Extension directory (`$UsnJrnl`, `$Quota`, `$ObjId`, `$RmMetadata`) |
| `36-144-1` | `System Volume Information` | Windows OS metadata directory |
| `39` | `$UsnJrnl` | Change journal (`$J` at `39-128-3`, `$Max` at `39-128-4`) |
| `64-144-2` | `Users` | User profile directory tree |
| `74-144-2` | `System` | System log directory |
| `V/V 256` | `$OrphanFiles` | Virtual TSK container for parentless deleted files |
